Nissan Leaf lifetime emissions are around 29 tonnes of CO2-equivalent, whereas a comparable ICE vehicle emits approximately 57 tonnes of CO2-equivalent.
Base case BEV scenario shows total lifecycle emissions of approximately 19 tCO2e, while the ICEV exhibits a much higher total lifecycle emission of 41.9 tCO2e
High-GHG minerals case increases BEV lifecycle emissions to about 21.1 tCO2e, relative to the ICEV which is 41.9 tCO2e
BEVs emit roughly half the GHG over their lifetime compared to ICEVs when sold in 2023.
BEVs in 2035 will have emissions about two-and-a-half times lower than ICEVs.
BEVs in 2035 will have emissions about three times lower than ICEVs.
Well-to-tank emissions are expected to decrease significantly, by up to 75% in the Announced Pledges Scenario, due to cleaner electricity generation.

The overall environmental impact for pyrometallurgical recycling is reported as 2.17 x 10^-11 when recycling 1000 kg of batteries, versus a reported impact of -1.50 x 10^-12 for hydrometallurgical recycling.  The value of 2.32E-11 is the result of adding the absolute values of the two values.

The overall environmental impact for pyrometallurgical recycling is reported as 2.17 x 10^-11 when recycling 1000 kg of batteries, versus a reported impact of -1.50 x 10^-12 for hydrometallurgical recycling. The value of -2.32E-11 is the result of adding the absolute values of the two values and multiplying by -1.
Direct physical recycling offers about a 32% reduction in emissions compared to traditional methods, equal to 37.2 kg CO2-eq/kWh.
CO2 emissions from pyrometallurgical recycling are approximately 0.224 kg CO2 per battery pack.
Energy required for recycling one battery pack is approximately 7.64 kWh for pyrometallurgical recycling.
Energy required for recycling one battery pack is approximately 7.76 kWh for hydrometallurgical recycling.
